summary: Gruber reflects on RAM Doubler, a classic Mac OS extension from 25 years ago that literally doubled available RAM through compression and virtual memory techniques. He uses it as a lens to examine the tradeoffs of classic Mac OS's open architecture, which lacked protected memory but allowed extraordinary third-party modifications. While acknowledging the instability this caused, he argues the openness enabled amazing software that simply isn't possible on modern, more locked-down systems. The piece is ultimately a meditation on what was lost when computing became more secure but less hackable.
key_points: RAM Doubler worked by replacing the entire Mac OS memory manager with a third-party implementation — effectively running a fork of the OS
Classic Mac OS's lack of protected memory is generally seen as a deficiency, but it also enabled extraordinary third-party innovation
The simplicity of the extension model — copy one file to install, remove it to uninstall — remains remarkable from a UX perspective
There's an inherent tension between system openness/hackability and stability/security that modern OSes have resolved firmly in favor of the latter
money_quotes: The most amazing thing, in hindsight, isn't that compression and clever virtual memory techniques could double your memory — it's that Mac OS was so open that something as low-level as RAM Doubler was even possible.
Effectively, a Mac running RAM Doubler was running a fork of the OS — not just a subtle fork but a fork where the entire memory manager was written by a third party.
But that anything goes openness also resulted in tremendous opportunities for third-party software.
From a low-level computer science operating systems perspective, the classic Mac OS was dangerously primitive. But from a high-level user interface perspective, it remains amazing.
To install RAM Doubler — software that radically changed the way the OS worked — all you had to do was copy one file to the Extensions folder in your System folder.
Third-party extensions could be exasperating, yes, but they could also be amazing and just plain fun in ways that aren't possible today.
key_insight: Classic Mac OS's dangerous openness — the same architectural flaw that caused constant crashes — was also what made extraordinary third-party innovation like RAM Doubler possible, a tradeoff modern computing has decisively abandoned.
